subject: Physics
q: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>kg</mi></math> is tied to end of a massless string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the breaking tension of the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then maximum linear velocity, the stone can have without breaking the string, while rotating in horizontal plane, is:
question: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>kg</mi></math> is tied to end of a massless string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the breaking tension of the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then maximum linear velocity, the stone can have without breaking the string, while rotating in horizontal plane, is:
solution:
In horizontal circle, tension towards the centre of the circular path at any moment will provide the required centripetal force.

Therefore,

Tmax=mv2r⇒400=1×v21⇒v=20 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A boy playing on the roof of a $10 \mathrm{~m}$ high building throws a ball with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. How far from the throwing point will the ball be at the height of $10 \mathrm{~m}$ from the ground?
$$
\left[\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}, \cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]
$$
question:
A boy playing on the roof of a $10 \mathrm{~m}$ high building throws a ball with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. How far from the throwing point will the ball be at the height of $10 \mathrm{~m}$ from the ground?
$$
\left[\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}, \cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]
$$
solution:
From, the question if the horizontal distance is none other than the horizontal range on the level of the roof of building
$$
\text { Range }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{(10)^2 \sin (2 \times 30)}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{10 \times 10 \times \sqrt{3}}{2 \times 10}=8.66
$$

subject: Physics
q: Projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown at angles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with vertical respectively from top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high tower. If their times of flight are same, the ratio of their speeds of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is:
question: Projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown at angles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with vertical respectively from top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high tower. If their times of flight are same, the ratio of their speeds of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is:
solution:
For same time of flight, vertical component of velocity of projection must be same. Therefore,

vAsin45°=vBsin30°⇒vAvB=1212⇒vAvB=12

subject: Physics
q: If $x$ and $y$ coordinates of a projectile as a function of time $(t)$ are given as $24t$ and $43.6t-4.9t^2$, respectively, then the angle (in degrees) made by the projectile with horizontal when $t=2$ s is ______.
question: If $x$ and $y$ coordinates of a projectile as a function of time $(t)$ are given as $24t$ and $43.6t-4.9t^2$, respectively, then the angle (in degrees) made by the projectile with horizontal when $t=2$ s is ______.
solution:
The velocity components of the projectile are given by the derivatives of the position coordinates with respect to time.

$v_x = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(24t) = 24$

$v_y = \dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(43.6t - 4.9t^2) = 43.6 - 9.8t$

At $t = 2$ s, the velocity components are:

$v_x = 24$

$v_y = 43.6 - 9.8(2) = 43.6 - 19.6 = 24$

The angle $\theta$ made by the velocity vector with the horizontal is given by:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{v_y}{v_x} = \dfrac{24}{24} = 1$

$\theta = 45^{\circ}$

Answer: $45$

subject: Physics
q: A body starts falling freely from height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> hits an inclined plane in its path at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. As a result of this perfectly elastic impact, the direction of the velocity of the body becomes horizontal. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>H</mi><mi>h</mi></mfrac></math> for which the body will take the maximum time to reach the ground is _____.
question: A body starts falling freely from height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> hits an inclined plane in its path at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. As a result of this perfectly elastic impact, the direction of the velocity of the body becomes horizontal. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>H</mi><mi>h</mi></mfrac></math> for which the body will take the maximum time to reach the ground is _____.
solution:
Total time of flight =T can be written as, 

T=2hg+2H-hg

For maximum time =dTdh=0

⇒2g12h-12H-h=0

⇒H-h=h

⇒h=H2⇒Hh=2

subject: Physics
q: A projectile can have the same range $\mathrm{R}$ for two angles of projection. If $\mathrm{t}_1$ and $\mathrm{t}_2$ be the times of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is proportional to
question: A projectile can have the same range $\mathrm{R}$ for two angles of projection. If $\mathrm{t}_1$ and $\mathrm{t}_2$ be the times of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is proportional to
solution: $\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u}^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}^2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{g}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Two identical balls A and B thrown with same velocity 'u' at two different angles with horizontal attained the same range R. If A and B reached the maximum height h1 and h2 respectively, then R=4h1h2

Reason R: Product of said heights. h1h2=u2sin2θ2g·u2cos2θ2g
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Two identical balls A and B thrown with same velocity 'u' at two different angles with horizontal attained the same range R. If A and B reached the maximum height h1 and h2 respectively, then R=4h1h2

Reason R: Product of said heights. h1h2=u2sin2θ2g·u2cos2θ2g
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
solution:
Range of two balls are R1=u2sin2θ1g=R2=u2sin2θ2g.

Now, maximum height by both balls are h1=u2sin2θ12g andh2=u2cos2θ22g

∴h1h2=u2sin2θ2gu2cos2θ2g=u2sinθcosθ2g=R4

⇒R=4h1h2

Hence, both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving on a circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> such that the magnitudes of the tangential acceleration and centripetal acceleration are equal. The time taken by the car to complete first quarter of revolution, if it is moving with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is ______.
question: A car is moving on a circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> such that the magnitudes of the tangential acceleration and centripetal acceleration are equal. The time taken by the car to complete first quarter of revolution, if it is moving with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given:

Initial speed =54×10003600=15 m s-1

at=ac

⇒vdvdx=v2R⇒∫15vdvv=1R∫0xdx

⇒v=15exR

⇒dxdt=15exR

Distance covered in first quarter of revolution =2πR4=πR2.

Therefore,

∫0πR2e-xRdx=15∫0t0dt

⇒t0=401-e-π2

Hence, t=40.

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are fired from ground with same initial speeds from same point at angles $\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)$ and $\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)$ with horizontal direction. The ratio of their times of flights is
question: Two projectiles are fired from ground with same initial speeds from same point at angles $\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)$ and $\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)$ with horizontal direction. The ratio of their times of flights is
solution:
$\theta_1=45+\alpha ; \theta_2=45-\alpha$
Time of flight, $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\sin (45+\alpha)}{\sin (45-\alpha)}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha-\sin \alpha}=\frac{1+\tan \alpha}{1-\tan \alpha}$

subject: Physics
q: The speed of a swimmer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in still water. If the swimmer makes his strokes normal to the flow of river of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, he reaches a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> down the stream on the opposite bank. The speed of the river water is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The speed of a swimmer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in still water. If the swimmer makes his strokes normal to the flow of river of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, he reaches a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> down the stream on the opposite bank. The speed of the river water is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Let vr be velocity of river and vm be velocity of man.

Time taken by man to cross the river of width =1000 m is t=14 h.

Now, drift x=vr×t

⇒0.750=vr×14⇒vr=3 km h-1

subject: Physics
q: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
question: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
solution:
Maximum height is achieved when the projection is along the vertical direction,

Hmax=v22 g=136 m

Now, for maximum range, angle of projection should be 45°. Therefore,

Rmax=v2 g=2Hmax

=2136

=272 m

subject: Physics
q: A ball rolls off the top of a stairway with horizontal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math>. The steps are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> wide. The minimum velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> with which that ball just hits the step <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> of the stairway will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_______ [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].
question: A ball rolls off the top of a stairway with horizontal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math>. The steps are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> wide. The minimum velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> with which that ball just hits the step <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> of the stairway will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_______ [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].
solution:
The ball needs to just cross 4 steps to just hit 5th step

Therefore, horizontal range

R=4×0.1 m= 0.4 m

The formula to calculate the horizontal range is given by

R=ut   ...1

Similarly, the vertical height covered by the ball is given by

h=12gt2   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it follows that

4×0.1=12gt2⇒0.4=12 g0.4u2⇒u2=2⇒u=2 m s-1

Therefore, x=2.

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
question: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
solution:
The maximum range is given by, Rmax=u2sin245°g=u2 g.

For the range to be half of the maximum range,

Rmax2=u22 g=u2sin2θg

⇒sin2θ=12

⇒2θ=30°or 2θ=150°

⇒θ=15°,75°

Therefore, answer is 15 or 75

q:
In a spring gun having spring constant 100 N m-1 a small ball B of mass 100 g is put in its barrel (as shown in figure) by compressing the spring through 0.05 m. There should be a box placed at a distance d on the ground so that the ball falls in it. If the ball leaves the gun horizontally at a height of 2 m above the ground. The value of d is m.
g=10 m s-2
question:
In a spring gun having spring constant 100 N m-1 a small ball B of mass 100 g is put in its barrel (as shown in figure) by compressing the spring through 0.05 m. There should be a box placed at a distance d on the ground so that the ball falls in it. If the ball leaves the gun horizontally at a height of 2 m above the ground. The value of d is m.
g=10 m s-2
solution:
12kx2=12mv2
Kx2=mv2
v=xkm=0.051000.1=0.05×1010
v=0.510
From h=12gt2
t=2 h g=2×210=210
∴ d=vt=0.510×210=1 m

subject: Physics
q: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
question: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
solution:
Let the car turn of the highway at a distance ' $x$ ' from the point $\mathrm{M}$. So, $\mathrm{RM}=x$ And if speed of car in field is $v$, then time taken by the car to cover the distance $Q R=$ $Q M-x$ on the highway, $$ t_1=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v} $$ Time taken to travel the distance ' $R P^{\prime}$ in the field $$ t_2=\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$ Total time elapsed to move the car from $Q$ to $P$ $$ t=t_1+t_2=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v}+\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$
$$ \text { or } x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2^2-1}}=\frac{d}{\sqrt{3}} $$

subject: Physics
q: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R, but different maximum heights, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Which of the following is correct?
question: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R, but different maximum heights, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We get same range for angle of projections θ and  90-θ .
h=u2sin2θ2g
⇒h1=u2sin2θ2g,h2=u2cos2θ2g
h1h2=u4sin2θcos2θ4g2=4u4sin2θcos2θ16g2
=u2sin2θg2116=R216
∴    R2=16h1h2

subject: Physics
q: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
question: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
solution:
Horizontal range is maximum if the angle of projection is 45° and is given by Rmax=u2g

Here, Rmax=100 m

Then, 100=u2g  ⋯1

If the ball is thrown vertically upward, then the ball will attain the maximum height from the ground as Hmax=u22g

Hmax=1002=50 m

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is projected at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from horizontal with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the projectile at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> from the start will be:
question: A projectile is projected at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from horizontal with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the projectile at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> from the start will be:
solution:
It is given that v=40 m s-1.

The components of the velocity will be vy=vsin30°=40 m s-12=20 m s-1vx=vcos30°=4032 m s-1=203  m s-1

The time taken to reach maximum height is T=usin30°g=20 m s-110 m s-2=2 s

Hence, at T=2 s, as vy=0 m s-1 therefore vnet=vx=203 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q: A modern grand-prix racing car of mass m is travelling on a flat track in a circular arc of radius R with a speed v. If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and the track is μs, then the magnitude of negative lift FL acting downwards on the car is:
question: A modern grand-prix racing car of mass m is travelling on a flat track in a circular arc of radius R with a speed v. If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and the track is μs, then the magnitude of negative lift FL acting downwards on the car is:
solution:
μsN=mv2R

N=mv2μsR=mg+FL

FL=mv2μsR-mg

question: A uniform cylinder of mass M and radius R is to be pulled over a step of height a a <R by applying a force F at its centre  'O' perpendicular to the plane through the axes of the cylinder on the edge of the step (see figure). The minimum value of F required is :

Mg1-R-aR2

FR>Mgcosθ R

F>Mgcosθ

F>MgR2-(R-a)2R. 

⇒Mg1-R-aR2

subject: Physics
q: A ball projected from ground at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ just clears a wall in front. If point of projection is $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of wall and ball strikes the ground at a distance of $6 \mathrm{~m}$ on the other side of the wall, the height of the wall is :
question: A ball projected from ground at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ just clears a wall in front. If point of projection is $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of wall and ball strikes the ground at a distance of $6 \mathrm{~m}$ on the other side of the wall, the height of the wall is :
solution: As ball is projected at an angle $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal therefore Range $=4 \mathrm{H}$ or $10=4 \mathrm{H} \Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=\frac{10}{4}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ $(\because$ Range $=4 \mathrm{~m}+6 \mathrm{~m}=10 \mathrm{~m})$ Maximum height, $\mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{u}^2=\frac{\mathrm{H} \times 2 \mathrm{~g}}{\sin ^2 \theta}=\frac{2.5 \times 2 \times 10}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}=100$ or, $\mathrm{u}=\sqrt{100}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ Height of wall PA $$ \begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{OA} \tan \theta-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{OA})^2}{\mathrm{u}^2 \cos ^2 \theta} \\ & =4-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{10 \times 16}{10 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0}}}=2.4 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} $$

question: The angle of projection of a particle is measured from the vertical axis as $\phi$ and the maximum height reached by the particle is $h_m$. Here $h_m$ as function of $\phi$ can be presented as
solution: $\mathrm{H}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \cos ^2 \phi}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical bodies, projected with the same speed at two different angles cover the same horizontal range $R$. If the time of flight of these bodies are $5$ s and $10$ s, respectively, then the value of $R$ is ________ m. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
question: Two identical bodies, projected with the same speed at two different angles cover the same horizontal range $R$. If the time of flight of these bodies are $5$ s and $10$ s, respectively, then the value of $R$ is ________ m. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
For two projectiles to have the same horizontal range $R$ with the same initial speed $u$, their angles of projection must be complementary, i.e., $\theta$ and $90^\circ - \theta$.

The times of flight for these two angles are given by:
$t_1 = \dfrac{2u \sin \theta}{g}$
$t_2 = \dfrac{2u \cos \theta}{g}$

Multiplying $t_1$ and $t_2$, we get:
$t_1 t_2 = \left(\dfrac{2u \sin \theta}{g}\right) \left(\dfrac{2u \cos \theta}{g}\right) = \dfrac{2}{g} \left(\dfrac{u^2 (2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)}{g}\right)$

Since the horizontal range is $R = \dfrac{u^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}$, we can write:
$t_1 t_2 = \dfrac{2R}{g}$

Given $t_1 = 5$ s, $t_2 = 10$ s, and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, substituting these values:
$5 \times 10 = \dfrac{2R}{10}$

$50 = \dfrac{2R}{10}$

$2R = 500 \Rightarrow R = 250$ m

Answer: $250$

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies are projected from ground with same speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at two different angles with respect to horizontal. The bodies were found to have same range. If one of the body was projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, with horizontal then sum of the maximum heights, attained by the two projectiles, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Two bodies are projected from ground with same speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at two different angles with respect to horizontal. The bodies were found to have same range. If one of the body was projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, with horizontal then sum of the maximum heights, attained by the two projectiles, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
For two projectiles with same speed to have the same horizontal range, angle of projection will be θ1+θ2=90°.

Therefore, θ2=90°-60°=30°.

Now, required sum of maximum heights will be,

Hmax1+Hmax2=u2sin2θ12g+u2sin2θ22g

=4022014+34=80 m

subject: Physics
q: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities will be in the ratio of:
question: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities will be in the ratio of:
solution:
Given m1=m2 and r1r2=34

As centripetal force is given by F=mv2r, in order to have the same centripetal force, we can write

F1=F2⇒m1v12r1=m2v22r2⇒v1v2=r1r2=32

subject: Physics
q:
If the angular velocity of earth's spin is increased such that the bodies at the equator start floating, the duration of the day would be approximately :
(Take : g=10 ms-2, the radius of earth, R=6400×103 m, Take π=3.14)
question:
If the angular velocity of earth's spin is increased such that the bodies at the equator start floating, the duration of the day would be approximately :
(Take : g=10 ms-2, the radius of earth, R=6400×103 m, Take π=3.14)
solution:
For objects to float, 

mg=mω2R

ω= angular velocity of the earth.

R= Radius of earth

ω=gR   ...1

Duration of day =T

T=2πω   ...2

⇒T=2πRg

=2π6400×10310

⇒T60=83.775 minutes

≃84 minutes

q: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
question: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
solution: Both velocity vectors are of same magnitude therefore resultant would pass exactly midway through them θ=30°

subject: Physics
q: A body is projected from the ground at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. Its velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum height reached by the body during its motion is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A body is projected from the ground at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. Its velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum height reached by the body during its motion is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
After 2 s the velocity is 20 m s-1

⇒vx2+vy2=v2=400

⇒u2cos245°+usin45°-gt2=u22+u2-202=400

⇒u22+u22+400-402u=400

 ⇒u2=402u⇒u=402 m s-1

Now the maximum height of the projectile is given by,

H=u2sin2θ2g⇒H=160020×2×2=20 m

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
question: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Max}}=\frac{(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta)^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1}{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45-\alpha)}{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45+\alpha)} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{1-\sin 2 \alpha}{1+\sin 2 \alpha}\end{aligned}$

q: The trajectory of a projectile near the surface of the earth is given as <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it were launched at an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> with speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> then <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
question: The trajectory of a projectile near the surface of the earth is given as <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it were launched at an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> with speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> then <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
solution:
y=2x-9x2
Comparing above equation with general equation of trajectory of projectile y=xtan⁡θ1-xR
tan θ=2
⇒sin θ=25 or cos θ=15
θ=sin-125 or θ=cos-115
And R = 2/9
R=v02sin⁡2θg=29
After substituting the value for g and sin 2θ. We get v0=35 m/s

q: A disc is rolling without slipping on a surface. The radius of the disc is R. At t=0, the top most point on the disc is A as shown in figure. When the disc completes half of its rotation, the displacement of point A from its initial position is
question: A disc is rolling without slipping on a surface. The radius of the disc is R. At t=0, the top most point on the disc is A as shown in figure. When the disc completes half of its rotation, the displacement of point A from its initial position is
solution:
The displacement is the hypotenuse of the diameter and the distance travelled for half the rotation πR.

By using Pythagoras theorem,

Displacement of A=(2R)2+(πR)2

=Rπ2+4

subject: Physics
q:
A boy throws a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height $H$. If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of $H$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question:
A boy throws a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height $H$. If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of $H$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
For projectile motion at 45°, vertical component of initial velocity:
$v_{0y} = v_0 \sin 45° = \frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}$
Maximum height occurs at $t = 2$ s when $v_y = 0$:
$0 = v_{0y} - gt$
$\Rightarrow v_{0y} = 10 \times 2 = 20$ m/s
Thus $\frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 20$, so $v_0 = 20\sqrt{2}$ m/s
Height at landing time $t = 3$ s:
$H = v_{0y}t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = 20 \times 3 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 9$
$= 60 - 45 = 15$ m

subject: Physics
q: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
question: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
solution:
Range is same for complimentary angles. $\mathrm{T}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin (90-\theta)}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $R=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{u} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{g}} \text {. }
$$

subject: Physics
q: A butterfly is flying with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in north-east direction. Wind is slowly blowing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from north to south. The resultant displacement of the butterfly in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> seconds is:
question: A butterfly is flying with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in north-east direction. Wind is slowly blowing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from north to south. The resultant displacement of the butterfly in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> seconds is:
solution:
V→BW=42cos45i^+42sin45j^
=4i^+4j^
V→w=-j^
V→B=V→BW+V→w=4i^+3j^
S→B=V→B×t=(4i^+3j^)×3=12i^+9j^
S→B=(12)2+(9)2=15 m
